name: Dicky Wells
abstract: William Wells (June 10, 1907 or 1909 – November 12, 1985), known professionally as Dicky Wells (sometimes Dickie Wells), was an American jazz trombonist.

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: Count Basie and His Orchestra with Benny Carter and The Swing Masters: Swing Lives
Newport Jazz Festival: Count Basie and His Orchestra with Benny Carter and The Swing Masters: Swing Lives

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: Dicky Wells, he had his own sound. Benny Morton, trombone player, they all had, everybody had their own sound. There was nobody that played like each other. When you’d get up to play a solo, you could distinct who it was.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry Edison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: The musicians other than Frank Newton that I did not know were Dickie Wells, trombone... I was with a bunch of musicians... Dickie Wells, Mack Walker, Harold McFerran, John William and myself ate in restaurants.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: I recorded with Dicky Wells' Orchestra... Dicky and I did three other numbers... I played this session with Dicky Wells... I was happy playing with my ole buddy Dickie Wells

about_quotes.speaker: Stanley Dance
about_quotes.evidence: I’d previously made one with Dickie Wells, the trombone player, and three other trombones, four trombones and a rhythm section, which was unusual but was successful
about_quotes.doc_title: Stanley Dance interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 12, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: William Randolph "Cozy" Cole
about_quotes.evidence: Trombones was Big Green, trombone, Dicky Wells and Keg Johnson. The rhythm section was Ernest Hill, Lawrence Lucie, guitar, Teddy Wilson and me.

about_quotes.speaker: Vic Dickenson
about_quotes.evidence: In the band with Basie we had a good trombone player, Stan Minor... and Dickie Wells... And Dickie Wells played a lot of lead too
